summary: Debugging Claude Code context losses, fixing design spec

message.content:
Precisamos urgente concluir o plano para criação da demonstração para o cliente, porém por estouros de contexto e de memoria estamos tendo moitos problemas com o claude code. Verifique o harness e de continuidade, analise os ultimos ultimos commits no git e as ultimas conversas do claude code referentes a este projeto salvas
  (~/.claude/projects/......) (ver todas!!! dos ultimos 3 dias, lendo todas as mensagens do usuário, porém as do claude devem ser lidas por demanda/necessidade
  porque são longas) para entender o que foi feito corretamente, alucinacões e percas por estouro de contexto. use a ferramenta de query em json via linha de comando jq para ler TODAS (eu disse todas, não apenas algumas) - Ver todas dos ultimos 3 dias, não esqueça.

Vou começar lendo o harness e depois verificar os commits e as conversas.

# Metro Integrated Planning System - Progress Log

## Current Task: DESIGN-REVISION - Critical Document Revision

### Status: IN PROGRESS - Executing Revision

---

## Session: 2025-12-20 14:XX (Continuation After Recovery)

### Task: Execute DESIGN-REVISION based on analysis

**Context Recovery Complete.** Analysis of previous sessions revealed:

| Issue | Finding |
|-------|---------|
| Context Loss | 91% of progress.txt lost due to late saves |
| Hallucination | DESIGN_SPEC.md says "Astro.js" but project uses Vite+React18 |
| Missing | Sandbox Mode under-specified |
| Missing | Wrong reference images (geospatial instead of Gantt) |

**Immediate Actions:**
1. ✅ Read and analyze conversation logs
2. ✅ Verified harness state
3. 🔄 Executing DESIGN-REVISION subtasks

**Subtasks (from features.json):**
- [ ] 1. Correct tech stack throughout document
- [ ] 2. Fetch new reference screenshots
- [ ] 3. Rewrite Moodboard section
- [ ] 4. Add detailed Sandbox Mode specification
- [ ] 5. Update file structure to reality
- [ ] 6. Add roadmap section
- [ ] 7. Simplify Storybook phases

---

## Session: 2025-12-20 (Harness Analysis & Recovery)

### Task: Analyze context loss and improve harness protocol

**Objective:** Analyze last 2 commits and Claude logs to understand what was done correctly, hallucinations, and context loss.

### Findings

#### 1. Context Loss Analysis

| Metric | Before | After | Lost |
|--------|--------|-------|------|
| claude-progress.txt | 2338 lines | 205 lines | **91%** |
| features.json | 392 lines | 142 lines | **64%** |

**Root Cause:** Sessions saved to harness only at the end, not incrementally.

#### 2. Hallucinations in DESIGN_SPEC.md

| Issue | Expected | Found |
|-------|----------|-------|
| Tech Stack | Vite + React 18 | "Astro.js 4.x" |
| References | Timeline/Gantt examples | Geospatial maps (irrelevant) |
| Sandbox Mode | Detailed state machine | Vague description |

#### 3. What Was Done Correctly

- ✅ Created DESIGN_SPEC.md (~1500 lines)
- ✅ Identified critical issues in analysis
- ✅ Saved plan to `.claude/plans/elegant-sparking-blossom.md`
- ✅ Created backup in `docs/archive/`
- ✅ Identified new reference sources (Epicflow, Dime Scheduler, AVS)

#### 4. POCs That Exist (from backup)

| POC | Location | Status |
|-----|----------|--------|
| XState Viewport | `stories/01-POCs/State/XStateViewport.stories.tsx` | ✅ |
| Semantic Zoom | `stories/01-POCs/Rendering/SemanticZoom.stories.tsx` | ✅ |
| Time Scrubber (7 variants) | `stories/01-POCs/TimeScrubber/` | ✅ |
| deck.gl Heatmap | `stories/01-POCs/Rendering/` | ✅ |
| FractalCanvas | `stories/FractalCanvas*.tsx` | ✅ |

### New Harness Rules Added

1. Save BEFORE starting any task
2. Save AFTER each subtask
3. Checkpoint every 15-20 minutes
4. Save before intensive operations
5. If context > 70%, STOP and save

### Anthropic Harness Research (Completed)

Source: [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)

**Key Learnings Applied:**
- JSON preferred for state files (less likely to be overwritten)
- Failure pattern 1: Doing too much → context exhaustion
- Failure pattern 2: Declaring done prematurely
- Incremental commits enable recovery
- Session startup rituals: always read context before acting

### Next Steps

1. Continue with DESIGN-REVISION task
2. Fetch new reference screenshots
3. Correct tech stack in DESIGN_SPEC.md

**Status:** Harness protocol improved ✅

---

## Session: 2025-12-19 (Critical Analysis)

### Executive Summary

The DESIGN_SPEC.md document has **CRITICAL issues** that must be resolved before implementation. A thorough analysis was completed and new reference sources were identified by the user.

---

## 🔴 CRITICAL ISSUES FOUND

### Issue 1: Wrong Reference Images

The downloaded reference images in `design/references/` have LOW implementation value:

| Image | Rating | Problem |
|-------|--------|---------|
| deck.gl/kepler-gl.jpg | 1/5 | Geospatial map - NOT timeline |
| deck.gl/flowmap.jpg | 1/5 | Flow visualization - NOT Gantt |
| deck.gl/sanddance.jpg | 1/5 | 3D statistical viz - irrelevant |
| linear/screenshots | 1-3/5 | Marketing pages, not UI patterns |
| railnova/railfleet.png | 2/5 | Splash page, not actual software |

The existing `design/screen-*.png` files (AI-generated) serve as inspiration only, not specification.

### Issue 2: Technology Stack Mismatch

Document says "Astro.js 4.x" but project uses Vite + React 18.

**Correct Stack:**
- Vite 5.x (NOT Astro)
- React 18.3.1
- deck.gl 9.2.5
- XState 5.x
- Tailwind CSS

### Issue 3: Sandbox Mode Underspecified

Sandbox Mode is the PRIMARY feature (addresses RFQ pain point #7 "No predictive simulations") but specification is vague. Missing:
- State machine diagram
- Persistence strategy
- Comparison algorithm
- Undo/redo implementation
- Conflict detection rules

### Issue 4: RFQ Pain Points Not Visually Addressed

Many solutions are abstract without concrete specifications.

### Issue 5: File Structure Mismatch

Document assumes Astro structure but project uses `apps/canvas/src/`.

---

## 🎯 NEW REFERENCE SOURCES (Critical - From User)

### Primary References (MUST Study)

| Source | URL | Purpose |
|--------|-----|---------|
| **Epicflow What-If** | https://www.epicflow.com/features/what-if/ | Sandbox Mode UI pattern |
| **Epicflow Pipeline** | https://www.epicflow.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/pipeline-implement.png | Visual planning layout |
| **Dime Scheduler** | https://www.dimescheduler.com/ | EXCELLENT - watch video! |
| **AVS Auto Demo** | https://avs.auto/demo/index.html | Single pane of glass example |
| **DHTMLX Gantt D3** | https://dhtmlx.github.io/demo-gantt-d3/index_1.html | Gantt with deck.gl-like rendering |
| **Prometheus Group** | https://www.prometheusgroup.com/resources/posts/what-does-flipping-burgers-have-to-do-with-managing-asset-or-location-availability | Asset availability patterns |
| **IBM Maximo Scheduler** | Search: "IBM Maximo Maintenance Scheduler" | Industry standard reference |

### Design Philosophy Sources

| Source | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| **Linear App** | Modern aesthetic, dark mode, typography |
| **JetBrains Fleet (Islands UI)** | Enterprise layout density with clarity |
| **Google Maps** | Navigation fluidity (zoom/drag paradigm) |

### Screenshots to Capture (Next Session)

```
1. https://www.epicflow.com/features/what-if/ (what-if toggle, delta panel)
2. https://www.epicflow.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/pipeline-implement.png
3. https://www.dimescheduler.com/ (video + screenshots)
4. https://avs.auto/demo/index.html (single pane example)
5. https://dhtmlx.github.io/demo-gantt-d3/index_1.html
6. Google Image Search: "IBM Maximo Maintenance Scheduler"
```

---

## User Decisions (Confirmed 2025-12-19)

1. **Tech Stack**: Vite + React 18 (correct from Astro.js)
2. **Sandbox Mode**: Detailed specification required (will be implemented later)
3. **Scope**: Planning View + Sandbox Mode focused, with roadmap for post-demo features
4. **References**: Use new high-quality sources, screen-*.png as inspiration only
5. **Design Goal**: Modern like Linear, but solid/corporate (not amateur). JetBrains Islands UI is good example.

---

## Revised Document Structure

### Part 1: Vision & RFQ Mapping
- Pain points → visual solutions → screen reference
- Demo focus: Planning View + Sandbox Mode
- Post-demo roadmap

### Part 2: Moodboard (REWRITE NEEDED)
**Primary Sources:**
- Epicflow (what-if, pipeline)
- Dime Scheduler (enterprise scheduling)
- AVS Demo (single pane)
- DHTMLX Gantt D3 (rendering)

**Secondary Sources:**
- Linear (aesthetic)
- JetBrains Fleet (Islands layout)
- screen-*.png (internal inspiration)

**Remove/Demote:**
- deck.gl geospatial images (irrelevant)
- Railnova splash page
- Linear marketing pages

### Part 3: Design System (Keep)
### Part 4: Application Shell (Update tech stack)
### Part 5: Planning View (Enhance with LOD references)
### Part 6: Sandbox Mode (MAJOR REWRITE with state machine)
### Part 7: Components (Keep)
### Part 8: Data Structures (Enhance)
### Part 9: Implementation (REWRITE tech stack)
### Part 10: Roadmap (NEW section)

---

## Demo Focus

### MUST Work Perfectly
1. Semantic Zoom (MACRO→MESO→MICRO)
2. Sandbox Mode Toggle (violet accent)
3. Delta Metrics (impact visualization)
4. Visual polish (modern, not amateur)

### Can Be Mock/Fake
1. Mileage calculations (hardcoded)
2. Reports
3. Notifications
4. Maximo integration

---

## Next Steps (Next Session)

1. **Fetch new reference screenshots** from Epicflow, Dime Scheduler, AVS, DHTMLX, IBM Maximo
2. **Rewrite Moodboard section** with new sources
3. **Correct tech stack** throughout document (Astro → Vite + React 18)
4. **Add detailed Sandbox Mode specification**:
   - XState state machine diagram
   - localStorage persistence schema
   - Comparison algorithm
   - Undo/redo stack
   - Conflict detection rules
5. **Update file structure** to match reality
6. **Add roadmap section** for post-demo features
7. **Simplify Storybook phases** for demo focus

---

## Analysis Sources

Three exploration agents completed analysis:

1. **DESIGN_SPEC.md Critical Analysis**: Found CRITICAL issues with tech stack mismatch, vague Sandbox Mode, abstract RFQ solutions
2. **Reference Images Value Analysis**: Rated screen-*.png as 5/5 (gold standard), deck.gl images as 1/5 (irrelevant)
3. **Better Reference Research**: Found Epicflow, Dime Scheduler, Prometheus Group, IBM Maximo, MRO software

---

## Files Modified This Session

| File | Action |
|------|--------|
| `.claude/plans/elegant-sparking-blossom.md` | Updated with full revision plan |
| `claude-progress.txt` | Updated with analysis results |

---

{
  "project": "Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "lastUpdated": "2025-12-20",

  },

      "passes": false,

  "demoFocus": {
    "mustWork": [
      "Semantic Zoom (MACRO→MESO→MICRO)",
      "Sandbox Mode Toggle (violet accent)",
      "Delta Metrics (impact visualization)",
      "Visual polish (modern, not amateur)"
    ],
    "canBeFake": [
      "Mileage calculations (hardcoded)",

      "Notifications",
      "Maximo integration"
    ]
  },
  "userDecisions": {

  }

input.command: cd ~/.claude/projects/-Users-anotonio-silva-antonio-mms-deck && for f in *.jsonl; do mtime=$(stat -f "%Sm" -t "%Y-%m-%d" "$f" 2>/dev/null); if [[ "$mtime" > "2025-12-17" ]]; then size=$(stat -f%z "$f" 2>/dev/null); if [ "$size" -gt 1000 ]; then echo "=== $f ($mtime, $(numfmt --to=iec $size 2>/dev/null || echo "${size}B")) ==="; fi; fi; done

input.description: Extrair mensagens do usuário da conversa Dec 20 (maior)

content.content: Olá Claude! Precisamos criar uma versão final da parte visual do app para apresentar nossa proposta ao cliente e ganharmos o processo de licitação. O mais importante é impressionarmos o cliente visualmente e interativamente com nosso frontend, de modo a demonstrar que podemos solucionar de forma excepcional as dores que ele reportou na RFQ (./docs/RFQ.md). Objetivo: Sua missão é criar livremente um documento de UX/Design/Produto que irá descrever a versão final do produto. Este documento é para uso interno - não será apresentado aos clientes. A próxima tarefa será, através deste documento, a implementação do frontend interativo com Astro.js e deck.gl, utilizando mocks para os dados. Portanto, o documento deve ser claro e completo com essa finalidade. Formato do documento: - Usar formato .md com o documento escrito em inglês - Incluir um Moodboard com das referências reais (não apenas links) - Para cada decisão de tela/solução, incluir imagens de exemplo de como deverá ficar, usando screenshots de apps concorrentes ou outras referências Fontes e Referências: - A fonte da verdade é exclusivamente a RFQ (docs/RFQ.md) - não considerar logicamente ou tecnicamente nenhum outro documento ou código no projeto - Os demais documentos do projeto podem ser lidos apenas como complemento de pesquisa - acessar as fontes originais e verificar se cada dado se enquadra no novo planejamento - Fazer extensa pesquisa na internet para referências visuais e soluções Referências de Design: - Usar o Linear como referência principal de design, especialmente a timeline - O "mood" do Linear (espaçamentos, cores, tipografia) serve de referência geral, mas não as soluções, por se tratar de problemas diferentes - Importante: Diferenciar claramente no documento o que é inspirado no Linear vs. o que é inspirado em apps de scheduling, manutenção, ferrovias, etc. - para evitar confusão durante as seções de implementação Tela Principal: - A tela mais importante é a de planejamento/visão dos dados com a implementação do modo "sandbox" que servirá para resolver a solicitação de "what-if scenarios" Tecnologia: - Está decidido e testado que o framework final será deck.gl - Não implementaremos manualmente features que podem causar problemas cross-browser (zoom, pan, pitch, etc.) - devemos utilizar as melhores opções disponíveis para trabalhar com deck.gl Harness: - Utilizar o sistema harness para salvaguardar progresso em caso de necessidade de continuarmos em nova sessão - Registrar o progresso e plano dessa task no harness Utilize modo planejamento e pensamento profundo. Inicie. The system 15→should offer an automated, data-driven, and real-time mileage management approach, considering 16→different preventive maintenance activities, current resources, and operational needs. The requirements 17→can be divided into three main categories. 18→●​ Maintenance Planning 19→●​ Operational Planning 20→●​ Resource Planning 21→Maintenance Planning: Key requirement to meet the maintenance planning, 22→●​ Fetching daily mileage for each train from a specific location. 23→●​ Predicting maintenance schedules and dates based on real-time mileage data. 24→●​ Ability to adjust the maintenance schedule based on available resources (man-hours), train 25→availability, and maintenance tracks. 26→●​ The software should be capable of providing short-term (weekly, monthly) and long-term 27→(yearly 1-10) plans based on the fixed parameters (man-hours and tracks). 28→●​ Offer guidance and warnings on mileage underrun or overrun, as they can impact overall 29→mileage management. 30→●​ Availability to perform simulations on various planning and scheduling options. 31→ 32→Operation Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the operational planning 33→●​ Recommend the train numbers to be used daily based on the fitness status and mileage. 34→●​ Recommend the run assignment (mileage and the network) for each train. 35→●​ Provide warnings and recommendations to correct or adjust fleet mileage by comparing the 36→predicted mileage with the actual mileage. 37→●​ Provide an interface between the maintenance and operations teams to communicate on 38→train fitness and mileage management. 39→ Resource Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the Resource planning 40→●​ The resource planning should consider the manpower, spare costs and tracks available, 41→which are fixed parameters. 42→●​ The maintenance planning should consider the fixed parameters and avoid overutilization or 43→underutilization of the resources throughout the contract period (12 years) 44→●​ The software should be able to provide the maintenance costing for each fleet based on the 45→mileage and maintenance plan. 46→ 47→ 48→## Page 2 49→ 50→●​ It should be able to provide different models or strategies in mileage management reflecting 51→the changes in total maintenance costs in the short term and long term. 52→2.​ Background 53→●​ 54→At present, no dedicated tools are available for mileage management. 55→●​ 56→The existing platform, IBM Maximo, primarily serves as an asset management tool and is 57→not specifically for managing mileage. 58→●​ 59→Currently, mileage management is being managed manually through Excel. 60→●​ 61→Existing setup currently depends on raw mileage data that is stored and collected 62→manually (folder driven mileage data), which requires validation and system integration. 63→●​ 64→Different fleet types and importantly Increasing fleet size necessitate a centralized, 65→automated solution to ensure better scheduling and compliance. 66→●​ 67→Limited integration between maintenance and operations on mileage decisions. 68→●​ 69→Risk of non-compliance due to mileage underrun/overrun as it is performed manually. 70→●​ 71→Lack of predictive simulations to evaluate planning options. 72→●​ 73→Insufficient visibility of resources vs. actual utilization. 74→●​ 75→Manual handling of data from multiple sources resulting in delays and inefficiencies. 76→ 77→3.​ Software’s core deliverables 78→The Mileage Management shall be capable of integrating, processing, and utilizing inputs from 79→maintenance, operations, and other required input, ensuring an automated, data-driven, and rule-based 80→approach. 81→●​ 82→Daily Mileage Data 83→●​ 84→Maintenance Plans 85→●​ 86→Maintenance Resources: 87→o​ Man-hours (fixed parameter). 88→o​ Track allocation for maintenance activities. 89→o​ Spare parts availability and baseline cost. 90→●​ 91→Fleet Identification: Train IDs and fleet classification (Kinki Sharyo / Alstom) 92→●​ 93→Fleet availability and Train Fitness Status 94→●​ 95→Mileage Balance Monitoring based on recommended run assignment. 96→●​ 97→Daily train deployment requirements across the network. 98→●​ 99→Adjustments between predicted vs. actual mileage utilization. 100→●​ 101→Maintenance budget forecasting 102→ 103→4.​ Software input 104→●​ Train IDs and fleet type (Kinki Sharyo and Alstom) 105→●​ Daily Mileage – Live data 106→●​ Daily train fitness status 107→●​ Daily operation requirement and command for each train run IDs 108→●​ Maintenance plans (e.g. PM, overhauls) 109→ 110→ 111→## Page 3 112→ 113→●​ Track availability 114→●​ Man-hours per maintenance 115→●​ Maintenance baseline cost per maintenance – material cost, service cost 116→●​ Man-hour cost 117→●​ Base Depot for each fleet (Identification of the designated maintenance depot for each 118→fleet) 119→ 120→5.​ Software output 121→●​ Centralised dashboard – This should provide one high level customised output and other 122→comprises complete data, mileage status 123→●​ Current maintenance status of all trains in terms of mileage 124→●​ Train run assignments (Optimised allocation of trains for daily service based on fitness 125→and mileage balance) 126→●​ Completed and upcoming PM/overhaul activities (Predicted schedule of upcoming 127→preventive maintenance and overhauls) 128→●​ Maintenance compliance status (Real-time tracking of adherence to maintenance 129→requirements based on run assignments) 130→●​ Monthly and yearly cost compliance (Comparison of actual vs. planned maintenance 131→costs on a monthly and yearly basis) 132→●​ Standard and custom report generation (PDF, Excel) 133→●​ Simulation report of different models on mileage management 134→●​ Email notification of the generated reports to the stakeholders 135→ 136→6.​ User Interface 137→●​ Web-based application (preferred) accessible on desktops and mobile devices. 138→●​ Capability to integrate with MMS (Maximo IBM) 139→●​ Data security and compliance with IT standards. 140→●​ Scalable architecture to accommodate additional fleets and extended functionalities. 141→●​ Customizable alerts/notifications 142→●​ Full accessibility as an admin. 143→ 144→7.​ Implementation support 145→●​ Project implementation methodology and timeline. 146→●​ Case studies or references of similar implementations. 147→●​ Supply, installation, and commissioning of the Mileage Control Management software. 148→●​ Warranty and post-deployment support, including upgradation as per regular software 149→updates and needs. 150→●​ Migration of existing maintenance data into the system. 151→●​ User training and knowledge transfer (Comprehensive training sessions for system users 152→to ensure smooth adoption) 153→ 154→ 155→## Page 4 156→ 157→●​ Documentation (Detailed technical and user manuals covering all functionalities) 158→ 159→8.​ Case studies 160→ Example: Workload (Man-hour projection required vs available): 161→ 162→a) Without adjustment: 163→ 164→ 165→b) With adjustment 166→ 167→ 168→9.​ Phase deployment 169→ 170→●​ Phase 1 – with regards to complete requirements as outlines above, phase 1 should have 171→implementation stage to cover the mileage management and run assignments. 172→●​ Phase 2 – This phase where the costing part is included and required output is driven. 173→ 174→ 175→## Page 5 176→ toolUseResult.stdout: Olá Claude! Precisamos criar uma versão final da parte visual do app para apresentar nossa proposta ao cliente e ganharmos o processo de licitação. O mais importante é impressionarmos o cliente visualmente e interativamente com nosso frontend, de modo a demonstrar que podemos solucionar de forma excepcional as dores que ele reportou na RFQ (./docs/RFQ.md). Objetivo: Sua missão é criar livremente um documento de UX/Design/Produto que irá descrever a versão final do produto. Este documento é para uso interno - não será apresentado aos clientes. A próxima tarefa será, através deste documento, a implementação do frontend interativo com Astro.js e deck.gl, utilizando mocks para os dados. Portanto, o documento deve ser claro e completo com essa finalidade. Formato do documento: - Usar formato .md com o documento escrito em inglês - Incluir um Moodboard com das referências reais (não apenas links) - Para cada decisão de tela/solução, incluir imagens de exemplo de como deverá ficar, usando screenshots de apps concorrentes ou outras referências Fontes e Referências: - A fonte da verdade é exclusivamente a RFQ (docs/RFQ.md) - não considerar logicamente ou tecnicamente nenhum outro documento ou código no projeto - Os demais documentos do projeto podem ser lidos apenas como complemento de pesquisa - acessar as fontes originais e verificar se cada dado se enquadra no novo planejamento - Fazer extensa pesquisa na internet para referências visuais e soluções Referências de Design: - Usar o Linear como referência principal de design, especialmente a timeline - O "mood" do Linear (espaçamentos, cores, tipografia) serve de referência geral, mas não as soluções, por se tratar de problemas diferentes - Importante: Diferenciar claramente no documento o que é inspirado no Linear vs. o que é inspirado em apps de scheduling, manutenção, ferrovias, etc. - para evitar confusão durante as seções de implementação Tela Principal: - A tela mais importante é a de planejamento/visão dos dados com a implementação do modo "sandbox" que servirá para resolver a solicitação de "what-if scenarios" Tecnologia: - Está decidido e testado que o framework final será deck.gl - Não implementaremos manualmente features que podem causar problemas cross-browser (zoom, pan, pitch, etc.) - devemos utilizar as melhores opções disponíveis para trabalhar com deck.gl Harness: - Utilizar o sistema harness para salvaguardar progresso em caso de necessidade de continuarmos em nova sessão - Registrar o progresso e plano dessa task no harness Utilize modo planejamento e pensamento profundo. Inicie. content.content: Olá Claude! Precisamos criar uma versão final da parte visual do app para apresentar nossa proposta ao cliente e ganharmos o processo de licitacao. O mais importante é impressionarmos o cliente visualmente e interativamente com nosso frontend, de modo a demonstrar que podemos solucionar de forma exceptional as dores que ele reportou na RFQ (./docs/RFQ.md). Sua missão é livremente criar um documento de UX/Design/Produto que irá descrever a versão final do produto, incluindo no documento imagens de referencia - usaremos .md e você deve incluir um Moodboard com das referencias reais, o mesmo vale para as decisões de como serão todas as outras solucoes, como você não irá desenhar realmente nesta tarefa, você incluirá imagens de exemplo de como deverá ficar a tela, as imagens podem ser de telas de apps concorrentes ou outras referencias (usar , não incluir apenas links) - A fonte da verdade é a RFQ e, embora você possa ler os demais documentos no projeto, deve considerar que muita coisa neles não ficou boa durante as POCs e precisamos de um plano final - está decidido e testado que o framework final será mesmo o deck.gl e que não implementaremos manualmente features que podem causar problemas cross-browser, como zoom, pan, pitch, etc - devemos utilizar as melhores opcoes disponiveis para se trabalhar com deck.gl dentro da nossa necessidade - você deve buscar na internet, fazer extensa pesquisa e utilizar o sistema harness para savaguardar em caso de necessidade de continuarmos em nova sessão. Utilize modo planejamento e pensamento profundo. Inicie. [ { "type": "tool_result", "content": "Entered plan mode. toolUseResult.stdout: Olá Claude! Precisamos criar uma versão final da parte visual do app para apresentar nossa proposta ao cliente e ganharmos o processo de licitacao. O mais importante é impressionarmos o cliente visualmente e interativamente com nosso frontend, de modo a demonstrar que podemos solucionar de forma exceptional as dores que ele reportou na RFQ (./docs/RFQ.md). Sua missão é livremente criar um documento de UX/Design/Produto que irá descrever a versão final do produto, incluindo no documento imagens de referencia - usaremos .md e você deve incluir um Moodboard com das referencias reais, o mesmo vale para as decisões de como serão todas as outras solucoes, como você não irá desenhar realmente nesta tarefa, você incluirá imagens de exemplo de como deverá ficar a tela, as imagens podem ser de telas de apps concorrentes ou outras referencias (usar , não incluir apenas links) - A fonte da verdade é a RFQ e, embora você possa ler os demais documentos no projeto, deve considerar que muita coisa neles não ficou boa durante as POCs e precisamos de um plano final - está decidido e testado que o framework final será mesmo o deck.gl e que não implementaremos manualmente features que podem causar problemas cross-browser, como zoom, pan, pitch, etc - devemos utilizar as melhores opcoes disponiveis para se trabalhar com deck.gl dentro da nossa necessidade - você deve buscar na internet, fazer extensa pesquisa e utilizar o sistema harness para savaguardar em caso de necessidade de continuarmos em nova sessão.     "content": "     1→# RFQ
     2→
     3→*Extracted from RFQ.pdf*
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Page 1
     8→
     9→ 
    10→RFQ Inputs 
    11→1.​ Scope of Work 
    12→Develop a solution that allows the maintenance and operations team to efficiently plan and schedule 
    13→preventative maintenance activities for two distinct rolling stock fleets, consisting of 49 and 79 trains 
    14→each (a total of 128 trains), while fulfilling operational requirements across the network. The system 
    15→should offer an automated, data-driven, and real-time mileage management approach, considering 
    16→different preventive maintenance activities, current resources, and operational needs. The requirements 
    17→can be divided into three main categories.  
    18→●​ Maintenance Planning 
    19→●​ Operational Planning 
    20→●​ Resource Planning  
    21→Maintenance Planning: Key requirement to meet the maintenance planning, 
    22→●​ Fetching daily mileage for each train from a specific location. 
    23→●​ Predicting maintenance schedules and dates based on real-time mileage data. 
    24→●​ Ability to adjust the maintenance schedule based on available resources (man-hours), train 
    25→availability, and maintenance tracks. 
    26→●​ The software should be capable of providing short-term (weekly, monthly) and long-term 
    27→(yearly 1-10) plans based on the fixed parameters (man-hours and tracks). 
    28→●​ Offer guidance and warnings on mileage underrun or overrun, as they can impact overall 
    29→mileage management. 
    30→●​ Availability to perform simulations on various planning and scheduling options. 
    31→ 
    32→Operation Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the operational planning 
    33→●​ Recommend the train numbers to be used daily based on the fitness status and mileage. 
    34→●​ Recommend the run assignment (mileage and the network) for each train. 
    35→●​ Provide warnings and recommendations to correct or adjust fleet mileage by comparing the 
    36→predicted mileage with the actual mileage. 
    37→●​ Provide an interface between the maintenance and operations teams to communicate on 
    38→train fitness and mileage management.  
    39→             Resource Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the Resource planning 
    40→●​ The resource planning should consider the manpower, spare costs and tracks available, 
    41→which are fixed parameters. 
    42→●​ The maintenance planning should consider the fixed parameters and avoid overutilization or 
    43→underutilization of the resources throughout the contract period (12 years) 
    44→●​ The software should be able to provide the maintenance costing for each fleet based on the 
    45→mileage and maintenance plan. 
    46→
    47→
    48→## Page 2
    49→
    50→●​ It should be able to provide different models or strategies in mileage management reflecting 
    51→the changes in total maintenance costs in the short term and long term. 
    52→2.​ Background 
    53→●​
    54→At present, no dedicated tools are available for mileage management. 
    55→●​
    56→The existing platform, IBM Maximo, primarily serves as an asset management tool and is 
    57→not specifically for managing mileage. 
    58→●​
    59→Currently, mileage management is being managed manually through Excel. 
    60→●​
    61→Existing setup currently depends on raw mileage data that is stored and collected 
    62→manually (folder driven mileage data), which requires validation and system integration. 
    63→●​
    64→Different fleet types and importantly Increasing fleet size necessitate a centralized, 
    65→automated solution to ensure better scheduling and compliance. 
    66→●​
    67→Limited integration between maintenance and operations on mileage decisions. 
    68→●​
    69→Risk of non-compliance due to mileage underrun/overrun as it is performed manually. 
    70→●​
    71→Lack of predictive simulations to evaluate planning options. 
    72→●​
    73→Insufficient visibility of resources vs. actual utilization. 
    74→●​
    75→Manual handling of data from multiple sources resulting in delays and inefficiencies. 
    76→ 
    77→3.​ Software’s core deliverables 
    78→The Mileage Management shall be capable of integrating, processing, and utilizing inputs from 
    79→maintenance, operations, and other required input, ensuring an automated, data-driven, and rule-based 
    80→approach. 
    81→●​
    82→Daily Mileage Data 
    83→●​
    84→Maintenance Plans 
    85→●​
    86→Maintenance Resources: 
    87→o​ Man-hours (fixed parameter). 
    88→o​ Track allocation for maintenance activities. 
    89→o​ Spare parts availability and baseline cost. 
    90→●​
    91→Fleet Identification: Train IDs and fleet classification (Kinki Sharyo / Alstom) 
    92→●​
    93→Fleet availability and Train Fitness Status 
    94→●​
    95→Mileage Balance Monitoring based on recommended run assignment. 
    96→●​
    97→Daily train deployment requirements across the network. 
    98→●​
    99→Adjustments between predicted vs. actual mileage utilization. 
   100→●​
   101→Maintenance budget forecasting 
   102→ 
   103→4.​ Software input 
   104→●​ Train IDs and fleet type (Kinki Sharyo and Alstom) 
   105→●​ Daily Mileage – Live data 
   106→●​ Daily train fitness status 
   107→●​ Daily operation requirement and command for each train run IDs 
   108→●​ Maintenance plans (e.g. PM, overhauls) 
   109→
   110→
   111→## Page 3
   112→
   113→●​ Track availability 
   114→●​ Man-hours per maintenance 
   115→●​ Maintenance baseline cost per maintenance – material cost, service cost 
   116→●​ Man-hour cost 
   117→●​ Base Depot for each fleet (Identification of the designated maintenance depot for each 
   118→fleet) 
   119→ 
   120→5.​ Software output 
   121→●​ Centralised dashboard – This should provide one high level customised output and other 
   122→comprises complete data, mileage status 
   123→●​ Current maintenance status of all trains in terms of mileage 
   124→●​ Train run assignments (Optimised allocation of trains for daily service based on fitness 
   125→and mileage balance) 
   126→●​ Completed and upcoming PM/overhaul activities (Predicted schedule of upcoming 
   127→preventive maintenance and overhauls) 
   128→●​ Maintenance compliance status (Real-time tracking of adherence to maintenance 
   129→requirements based on run assignments) 
   130→●​ Monthly and yearly cost compliance (Comparison of actual vs. planned maintenance 
   131→costs on a monthly and yearly basis) 
   132→●​ Standard and custom report generation (PDF, Excel) 
   133→●​ Simulation report of different models on mileage management 
   134→●​ Email notification of the generated reports to the stakeholders 
   135→ 
   136→6.​ User Interface 
   137→●​ Web-based application (preferred) accessible on desktops and mobile devices. 
   138→●​ Capability to integrate with MMS (Maximo IBM) 
   139→●​ Data security and compliance with IT standards. 
   140→●​ Scalable architecture to accommodate additional fleets and extended functionalities. 
   141→●​ Customizable alerts/notifications 
   142→●​ Full accessibility as an admin. 
   143→ 
   144→7.​ Implementation support 
   145→●​ Project implementation methodology and timeline. 
   146→●​ Case studies or references of similar implementations. 
   147→●​ Supply, installation, and commissioning of the Mileage Control Management software. 
   148→●​ Warranty and post-deployment support, including upgradation as per regular software 
   149→updates and needs. 
   150→●​ Migration of existing maintenance data into the system. 
   151→●​ User training and knowledge transfer (Comprehensive training sessions for system users 
   152→to ensure smooth adoption) 
   153→
   154→
   155→## Page 4
   156→
   157→●​ Documentation (Detailed technical and user manuals covering all functionalities) 
   158→ 
   159→8.​ Case studies

   161→

   163→ 
   164→ 
   165→b) With adjustment 
   166→ 
   167→ 
   168→9.​ Phase deployment 
   169→ 
   170→●​ Phase 1 – with regards to complete requirements as outlines above, phase 1 should have 
   171→implementation stage to cover the mileage management and run assignments. 
   172→●​ Phase 2 – This phase where the costing part is included and required output is driven. 
   173→
   174→
   175→## Page 5
   176→
   177→●​ Phase 3 – Inclusion of room for the additional fleet into the system for which system 
   178→retains the capabilities to do so. Competence Management part to be added to the 
   179→system which can regulate the competency of the user. 
   180→●​ Phase 3 – To be defined (Future scope)

    "content": "     1→# UX Research: Canvas Pan/Zoom Best Practices
     2→
     3→> Research conducted for UX-AUDIT-001 to inform F118-F121 implementation.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## 1. Industry Leader Patterns
     8→
     9→### 1.1 Figma
    10→
    11→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    12→|---------|--------|-------|
    13→| Scroll wheel | Pan vertically | Default behavior (browser compatibility) |
    14→| Ctrl/Cmd + Scroll | Zoom in/out | Standard modifier pattern |
    15→| Middle-click drag | Pan in any direction | Alternative pan gesture |
    16→| Two-finger trackpad | Pan (both axes) | Natural for laptop users |
    17→| Pinch trackpad | Zoom | Native gesture support |
    18→| Spacebar + drag | Pan | Classic design tool pattern |
    19→| Z key + click | Zoom to point | Precision zoom gesture |
    20→
    21→**Key Insight:** Figma made a deliberate compromise - scroll wheel pans vertically by default (browser-native behavior) rather than zooming. This prevents scroll hijacking on pages with embedded Figma content.
    22→
    23→**Customization:** Users can enable "Scroll wheel zoom" in Preferences, making scroll = zoom (without Ctrl).
    24→
    25→### 1.2 Google Maps
    26→
    27→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    28→|---------|--------|-------|
    29→| Drag | Pan with momentum | Velocity-based inertia |
    30→| Scroll wheel | Zoom | Centers on cursor |
    31→| Double-click | Zoom in 2x | At click location |
    32→| Pinch | Zoom | Touch devices |
    33→| Two-finger drag | Pan | Touch devices |
    34→
    35→**Gesture Handling Modes:**
    36→- `cooperative`: Scroll pans page; Ctrl+scroll zooms map
    37→- `greedy`: Scroll always zooms map
    38→- `none`: Disable interactions
    39→
    40→**Camera Easing:** Google uses `Quadratic.Out` easing with tween.js for smooth camera transitions. Typical duration: 300-500ms for zoom, 15s for animated tours.
    41→
    42→**Momentum Physics:**
    43→- Pan has momentum/inertia after release
    44→- Zoom does NOT have momentum (intentional UX decision)
    45→- Friction coefficient creates natural deceleration
    46→
    47→### 1.3 Apple Maps / iOS
    48→
    49→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    50→|---------|--------|-------|
    51→| One-finger drag | Pan with strong momentum | "Buttery" feel |
    52→| Pinch | Zoom with momentum | Unlike Google Maps |
    53→| Double-tap | Zoom in | Fixed 2x increment |
    54→| Two-finger tap | Zoom out | Fixed 0.5x increment |
    55→| Rotate gesture | Rotate map | Optional feature |
    56→
    57→**Key Differentiator:** iOS implements momentum for BOTH pan AND zoom gestures, creating a more fluid feel than Google Maps.
    58→
    59→### 1.4 Canva
    60→
    61→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    62→|---------|--------|-------|
    63→| Drag | Pan canvas | Infinite canvas |
    64→| Scroll wheel | Zoom | Centers on cursor |
    65→| Pinch | Zoom | Touch devices |
    66→| Pan gesture | Pan | Two-finger drag |
    67→
    68→**Infinite Canvas:** Canva's whiteboard feature supports unlimited pan in all directions with zoom/pan/scroll navigation.
    69→
    70→---
    71→
    72→## 2. Mouse Events Reference
    73→
    74→### 2.1 Event Types
    75→
    76→| Event | Triggers | Properties |
    77→|-------|----------|------------|
    78→| `mousedown` | Button pressed | `button`, `buttons`, `clientX/Y` |
    79→| `mouseup` | Button released | `button`, `buttons`, `clientX/Y` |
    80→| `mousemove` | Cursor moved | `clientX/Y`, `movementX/Y` |
    81→| `click` | Press + release (same element) | Fires after `mouseup` |
    82→| `dblclick` | Two clicks within threshold | ~300ms default |
    83→| `contextmenu` | Right-click | Prevent for custom menu |
    84→| `wheel` | Scroll wheel rotated | `deltaX/Y/Z`, `deltaMode` |
    85→| `mouseenter` | Cursor enters element | Does NOT bubble |
    86→| `mouseleave` | Cursor leaves element | Does NOT bubble |
    87→| `mouseover` | Cursor over element | Bubbles (includes children) |
    88→| `mouseout` | Cursor leaves element | Bubbles (includes children) |
    89→
    90→### 2.2 Button Values
    91→
    92→| `event.button` | Description |
    93→|----------------|-------------|
    94→| 0 | Left button (primary) |
    95→| 1 | Middle button (wheel click) |
    96→| 2 | Right button (secondary) |
    97→| 3 | Back button (browser) |
    98→| 4 | Forward button (browser) |
    99→
   100→### 2.3 Drag Implementation Pattern
   101→
   102→```typescript
   103→interface DragState {
   104→  isDragging: boolean;
   105→  startX: number;
   106→  startY: number;
   107→  lastX: number;
   108→  lastY: number;
   109→  velocityX: number;
   110→  velocityY: number;
   111→  lastTime: number;
   112→}
   113→
   114→// Calculate velocity during drag for momentum
   115→function onMouseMove(e: MouseEvent, state: DragState) {
   116→  const now = performance.now();
   117→  const dt = now - state.lastTime;
   118→
   119→  if (dt > 0) {
   120→    state.velocityX = (e.clientX - state.lastX) / dt;
   121→    state.velocityY = (e.clientY - state.lastY) / dt;
   122→  }
   123→
   124→  state.lastX = e.clientX;
   125→  state.lastY = e.clientY;
   126→  state.lastTime = now;
   127→}
   128→```
   129→
   130→### 2.4 Double-Click Detection
   131→
   132→```typescript
   133→const DOUBLE_CLICK_THRESHOLD = 300; // ms
   134→let lastClickTime = 0;
   135→
   136→function onClick(e: MouseEvent) {
   137→  const now = Date.now();
   138→  if (now - lastClickTime < DOUBLE_CLICK_THRESHOLD) {
   139→    handleDoubleClick(e);
   140→  }
   141→  lastClickTime = now;
   142→}
   143→```
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## 3. Touch Events Reference
   148→
   149→### 3.1 Touch Event Types
   150→
   151→| Event | Triggers | Notes |
   152→|-------|----------|-------|
   153→| `touchstart` | Finger touches screen | One or more touches |
   154→| `touchmove` | Finger moves on screen | Fires frequently |
   155→| `touchend` | Finger lifts from screen | |
   156→| `touchcancel` | Touch interrupted | System event, incoming call |
   157→
   158→### 3.2 Pointer Events (Modern, Unified)
   159→
   160→Pointer Events unify mouse, touch, and pen input:
   161→
   162→| Event | Equivalent Touch/Mouse |
   163→|-------|------------------------|
   164→| `pointerdown` | `touchstart` / `mousedown` |
   165→| `pointermove` | `touchmove` / `mousemove` |
   166→| `pointerup` | `touchend` / `mouseup` |
   167→| `pointercancel` | `touchcancel` |
   168→| `pointerenter` | `mouseenter` |
   169→| `pointerleave` | `mouseleave` |
   170→
   171→**Key Properties:**
   172→- `pointerId`: Unique identifier for each contact
   173→- `pointerType`: "mouse", "touch", "pen"
   174→- `isPrimary`: True for first finger/primary button
   175→- `pressure`: 0.0 to 1.0 (pen pressure)
   176→- `width`, `height`: Contact area size
   177→
   178→### 3.3 Pinch-to-Zoom Algorithm
   179→
   180→```typescript
   181→// Cache active pointers
   182→const evCache: PointerEvent[] = [];
   183→let prevDist = -1;
   184→
   185→function onPointerDown(ev: PointerEvent) {
   186→  evCache.push(ev);
   187→}
   188→
   189→function onPointerMove(ev: PointerEvent) {
   190→  // Update cached pointer
   191→  const idx = evCache.findIndex(e => e.pointerId === ev.pointerId);
   192→  if (idx >= 0) evCache[idx] = ev;
   193→
   194→  // Pinch gesture requires exactly 2 pointers
   195→  if (evCache.length === 2) {
   196→    const dx = evCache[0].clientX - evCache[1].clientX;
   197→    const dy = evCache[0].clientY - evCache[1].clientY;
   198→    const curDist = Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);
   199→
   200→    if (prevDist > 0) {
   201→      const scale = curDist / prevDist;
   202→      // scale > 1 = zoom in, scale < 1 = zoom out
   203→      handlePinchZoom(scale, getMidpoint(evCache[0], evCache[1]));
   204→    }
   205→
   206→    prevDist = curDist;
   207→  }
   208→}
   209→
   210→function onPointerUp(ev: PointerEvent) {
   211→  const idx = evCache.findIndex(e => e.pointerId === ev.pointerId);
   212→  if (idx >= 0) evCache.splice(idx, 1);
   213→  if (evCache.length < 2) prevDist = -1;
   214→}
   215→
   216→function getMidpoint(a: PointerEvent, b: PointerEvent) {
   217→  return {
   218→    x: (a.clientX + b.clientX) / 2,
   219→    y: (a.clientY + b.clientY) / 2
   220→  };
   221→}
   222→```
   223→
   224→### 3.4 Touch CSS Requirements
   225→
   226→```css
   227→/* Prevent default browser gestures */
   228→.canvas-container {
   229→  touch-action: none;        /* Full control over touch */
   230→  -webkit-user-select: none; /* Prevent text selection */
   231→  user-select: none;
   232→}
   233→```
   234→
   235→---
   236→
   237→## 4. Wheel Event Normalization
   238→
   239→### 4.1 The Problem
   240→
   241→Different browsers/platforms report wildly different `deltaY` values:
   242→
   243→| Environment | deltaY per "tick" |
   244→|-------------|-------------------|
   245→| Chrome (Windows) | 100-120 |
   246→| Chrome (macOS trackpad) | 1-5 (many events) |
   247→| Firefox (Windows) | 3 (lines mode) |
   248→| Firefox (macOS) | 1-2 |
   249→| Safari | 1-12 |
   250→
   251→### 4.2 deltaMode Values
   252→
   253→```typescript
   254→enum WheelDeltaMode {
   255→  DOM_DELTA_PIXEL = 0,  // deltaY is in pixels
   256→  DOM_DELTA_LINE = 1,   // deltaY is in lines
   257→  DOM_DELTA_PAGE = 2    // deltaY is in pages
   258→}
   259→```
   260→
   261→### 4.3 Facebook's Normalization Algorithm
   262→
   263→```typescript
   264→// Constants for normalization
   265→const PIXEL_STEP = 10;
   266→const LINE_HEIGHT = 40;
   267→const PAGE_HEIGHT = 800;
   268→
   269→interface NormalizedWheel {
   270→  spinX: number;   // Normalized spin (-1 to 1)
   271→  spinY: number;   // Normalized spin (-1 to 1)
   272→  pixelX: number;  // Normalized pixels
   273→  pixelY: number;  // Normalized pixels
   274→}
   275→
   276→function normalizeWheel(event: WheelEvent): NormalizedWheel {
   277→  let sX = 0, sY = 0;   // spin
   278→  let pX = 0, pY = 0;   // pixel
   279→
   280→  // Modern wheel event
   281→  if ('deltaY' in event) {
   282→    pX = event.deltaX;
   283→    pY = event.deltaY;
   284→
   285→    // Convert based on deltaMode
   286→    if (event.deltaMode === 1) {        // LINE mode
   287→      pX *= LINE_HEIGHT;
   288→      pY *= LINE_HEIGHT;
   289→    } else if (event.deltaMode === 2) { // PAGE mode
   290→      pX *= PAGE_HEIGHT;
   291→      pY *= PAGE_HEIGHT;
   292→    }
   293→  }
   294→
   295→  // Legacy fallback
   296→  if ('wheelDelta' in event) {
   297→    const wd = (event as any).wheelDelta;
   298→    if ('wheelDeltaY' in event) {
   299→      sY = -(event as any).wheelDeltaY / 120;
   300→      sX = -(event as any).wheelDeltaX / 120;
   301→    } else {
   302→      sY = -wd / 120;
   303→    }
   304→  }
   305→
   306→  // Calculate spin from pixel if not set
   307→  if (!sX && pX) sX = pX < 0 ? -1 : pX > 0 ? 1 : 0;
   308→  if (!sY && pY) sY = pY < 0 ? -1 : pY > 0 ? 1 : 0;
   309→
   310→  return { spinX: sX, spinY: sY, pixelX: pX, pixelY: pY };
   311→}
   312→```
   313→
   314→### 4.4 Trackpad vs Mouse Detection
   315→
   316→Trackpads produce many small events rapidly; mice produce fewer large events:
   317→
   318→```typescript
   319→let lastWheelTime = 0;
   320→let wheelEventCount = 0;
   321→
   322→function detectInputType(event: WheelEvent): 'trackpad' | 'mouse' {
   323→  const now = performance.now();
   324→  const timeDelta = now - lastWheelTime;
   325→
   326→  if (timeDelta < 50) {
   327→    wheelEventCount++;
   328→  } else {
   329→    wheelEventCount = 1;
   330→  }
   331→
   332→  lastWheelTime = now;
   333→
   334→  // Trackpads fire many events rapidly (<20ms apart)
   335→  // Mice fire discrete events with gaps
   336→  if (wheelEventCount > 3 && timeDelta < 20) {
   337→    return 'trackpad';
   338→  }
   339→
   340→  // Also check delta magnitude
   341→  if (Math.abs(event.deltaY) < 10 && event.deltaMode === 0) {
   342→    return 'trackpad'; // Small pixel deltas = trackpad
   343→  }
   344→
   345→  return 'mouse';
   346→}
   347→```
   348→
   349→---
   350→
   351→## 5. Momentum/Inertia Physics
   352→
   353→### 5.1 Basic Momentum Implementation
   354→
   355→```typescript
   356→interface MomentumState {
   357→  velocityX: number;
   358→  velocityY: number;
   359→  friction: number;
   360→  minVelocity: number;
   361→  animationId: number | null;
   362→}
   363→
   364→const momentum: MomentumState = {
   365→  velocityX: 0,
   366→  velocityY: 0,
   367→  friction: 0.95,      // Per-frame velocity multiplier
   368→  minVelocity: 0.1,    // Stop threshold
   369→  animationId: null
   370→};
   371→
   372→function startMomentum(vx: number, vy: number) {
   373→  momentum.velocityX = vx;
   374→  momentum.velocityY = vy;
   375→
   376→  if (momentum.animationId) {
   377→    cancelAnimationFrame(momentum.animationId);
   378→  }
   379→
   380→  momentum.animationId = requestAnimationFrame(animateMomentum);
   381→}
   382→
   383→function animateMomentum() {
   384→  // Apply velocity to pan position
   385→  panX += momentum.velocityX;
   386→  panY += momentum.velocityY;
   387→
   388→  // Apply friction
   389→  momentum.velocityX *= momentum.friction;
   390→  momentum.velocityY *= momentum.friction;
   391→
   392→  // Stop when velocity is negligible
   393→  const speed = Math.sqrt(
   394→    momentum.velocityX ** 2 + momentum.velocityY ** 2
   395→  );
   396→
   397→  if (speed > momentum.minVelocity) {
   398→    momentum.animationId = requestAnimationFrame(animateMomentum);
   399→  } else {
   400→    momentum.animationId = null;
   401→  }
   402→}
   403→
   404→function stopMomentum() {
   405→  if (momentum.animationId) {
   406→    cancelAnimationFrame(momentum.animationId);
   407→    momentum.animationId = null;
   408→  }
   409→  momentum.velocityX = 0;
   410→  momentum.velocityY = 0;
   411→}
   412→```
   413→
   414→### 5.2 Easing Functions
   415→
   416→```typescript
   417→// Common easing functions for zoom animations
   418→const Easing = {
   419→  // Quadratic ease out (Google Maps style)
   420→  quadOut: (t: number) => t * (2 - t),
   421→
   422→  // Cubic ease out (smoother)
   423→  cubicOut: (t: number) => 1 - Math.pow(1 - t, 3),
   424→
   425→  // Expo ease out (fast start, slow end)
   426→  expoOut: (t: number) => t === 1 ? 1 : 1 - Math.pow(2, -10 * t),
   427→
   428→  // Circ ease out (natural feel)
   429→  circOut: (t: number) => Math.sqrt(1 - Math.pow(t - 1, 2))
   430→};
   431→
   432→function animateZoom(
   433→  fromZoom: number,
   434→  toZoom: number,
   435→  duration: number,
   436→  easing = Easing.quadOut
   437→) {
   438→  const startTime = performance.now();
   439→
   440→  function tick(currentTime: number) {
   441→    const elapsed = currentTime - startTime;
   442→    const progress = Math.min(elapsed / duration, 1);
   443→    const easedProgress = easing(progress);
   444→
   445→    const currentZoom = fromZoom + (toZoom - fromZoom) * easedProgress;
   446→    setZoom(currentZoom);
   447→
   448→    if (progress < 1) {
   449→      requestAnimationFrame(tick);
   450→    }
   451→  }
   452→
   453→  requestAnimationFrame(tick);
   454→}
   455→```
   456→
   457→### 5.3 Recommended Durations
   458→
   459→| Animation | Duration | Easing |
   460→|-----------|----------|--------|
   461→| Zoom step (+/-) | 200-300ms | quadOut |
   462→| Double-click zoom | 300-400ms | cubicOut |
   463→| Reset view | 400-500ms | expoOut |
   464→| Pan deceleration | Physics-based | Friction 0.92-0.95 |
   465→
   466→---
   467→
   468→## 6. Cross-Platform Considerations
   469→
   470→### 6.1 macOS vs Windows Scroll Direction
   471→
   472→| Platform | Default Scroll Direction |
   473→|----------|--------------------------|
   474→| macOS (trackpad) | "Natural" (content follows fingers) |
   475→| macOS (mouse) | Same as trackpad (unless changed) |
   476→| Windows | "Traditional" (opposite of macOS) |
   477→| Linux | Varies by desktop environment |
   478→
   479→**Recommendation:** Use the raw `deltaY` value directly. Browser vendors have already normalized for user preferences. Do NOT attempt to detect or reverse scroll direction.
   480→
   481→### 6.2 Platform-Specific deltaY Magnitude
   482→
   483→| Platform | Typical deltaY (mouse) | Typical deltaY (trackpad) |
   484→|----------|------------------------|---------------------------|
   485→| Windows Chrome | 100 | 1-10 (many events) |
   486→| macOS Chrome | 100 | 0.5-3 (many events) |
   487→| Windows Firefox | 3 (LINE mode) | 1-2 |
   488→| macOS Safari | 12-30 | 0.5-2 |
   489→
   490→### 6.3 Recommended Normalization Strategy
   491→
   492→```typescript
   493→const ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.001;  // Base sensitivity
   494→const MAX_ZOOM_DELTA = 0.5;      // Cap per event
   495→
   496→function calculateZoomDelta(event: WheelEvent): number {
   497→  let delta = event.deltaY;
   498→
   499→  // Normalize LINE mode to pixels
   500→  if (event.deltaMode === 1) {
   501→    delta *= 16; // Approximate line height
   502→  }
   503→
   504→  // Apply sensitivity and clamp
   505→  delta *= ZOOM_SENSITIVITY;
   506→  delta = Math.max(-MAX_ZOOM_DELTA, Math.min(MAX_ZOOM_DELTA, delta));
   507→
   508→  return delta;
   509→}
   510→```

    11→1.​ Scope of Work 
    12→Develop a solution that allows the maintenance and operations team to efficiently plan and schedule 
    13→preventative maintenance activities for two distinct rolling stock fleets, consisting of 49 and 79 trains 
    14→each (a total of 128 trains), while fulfilling operational requirements across the network. The system 
    15→should offer an automated, data-driven, and real-time mileage management approach, considering 
    16→different preventive maintenance activities, current resources, and operational needs. The requirements 
    17→can be divided into three main categories.  
    18→●​ Maintenance Planning 
    19→●​ Operational Planning 
    20→●​ Resource Planning  
    21→Maintenance Planning: Key requirement to meet the maintenance planning, 
    22→●​ Fetching daily mileage for each train from a specific location. 
    23→●​ Predicting maintenance schedules and dates based on real-time mileage data. 
    24→●​ Ability to adjust the maintenance schedule based on available resources (man-hours), train 
    25→availability, and maintenance tracks. 
    26→●​ The software should be capable of providing short-term (weekly, monthly) and long-term 
    27→(yearly 1-10) plans based on the fixed parameters (man-hours and tracks). 
    28→●​ Offer guidance and warnings on mileage underrun or overrun, as they can impact overall 
    29→mileage management. 
    30→●​ Availability to perform simulations on various planning and scheduling options. 
    31→ 
    32→Operation Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the operational planning 
    33→●​ Recommend the train numbers to be used daily based on the fitness status and mileage. 
    34→●​ Recommend the run assignment (mileage and the network) for each train. 
    35→●​ Provide warnings and recommendations to correct or adjust fleet mileage by comparing the 
    36→predicted mileage with the actual mileage. 
    37→●​ Provide an interface between the maintenance and operations teams to communicate on 
    38→train fitness and mileage management.  
    39→             Resource Planning: Key requirement to fulfil the Resource planning 
    40→●​ The resource planning should consider the manpower, spare costs and tracks available,

     2→
     3→> Research conducted for UX-AUDIT-001 to inform F118-F121 implementation.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## 1. Industry Leader Patterns
     8→
     9→### 1.1 Figma
    10→
    11→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    12→|---------|--------|-------|
    13→| Scroll wheel | Pan vertically | Default behavior (browser compatibility) |
    14→| Ctrl/Cmd + Scroll | Zoom in/out | Standard modifier pattern |
    15→| Middle-click drag | Pan in any direction | Alternative pan gesture |
    16→| Two-finger trackpad | Pan (both axes) | Natural for laptop users |
    17→| Pinch trackpad | Zoom | Native gesture support |
    18→| Spacebar + drag | Pan | Classic design tool pattern |
    19→| Z key + click | Zoom to point | Precision zoom gesture |
    20→
    21→**Key Insight:** Figma made a deliberate compromise - scroll wheel pans vertically by default (browser-native behavior) rather than zooming. This prevents scroll hijacking on pages with embedded Figma content.
    22→
    23→**Customization:** Users can enable "Scroll wheel zoom" in Preferences, making scroll = zoom (without Ctrl).
    24→
    25→### 1.2 Google Maps
    26→
    27→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    28→|---------|--------|-------|
    29→| Drag | Pan with momentum | Velocity-based inertia |
    30→| Scroll wheel | Zoom | Centers on cursor |
    31→| Double-click | Zoom in 2x | At click location |
    32→| Pinch | Zoom | Touch devices |
    33→| Two-finger drag | Pan | Touch devices |
    34→
    35→**Gesture Handling Modes:**
    36→- `cooperative`: Scroll pans page; Ctrl+scroll zooms map
    37→- `greedy`: Scroll always zooms map
    38→- `none`: Disable interactions
    39→
    40→**Camera Easing:** Google uses `Quadratic.Out` easing with tween.js for smooth camera transitions. Typical duration: 300-500ms for zoom, 15s for animated tours.
    41→
    42→**Momentum Physics:**
    43→- Pan has momentum/inertia after release
    44→- Zoom does NOT have momentum (intentional UX decision)
    45→- Friction coefficient creates natural deceleration
    46→
    47→### 1.3 Apple Maps / iOS
    48→
    49→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    50→|---------|--------|-------|
    51→| One-finger drag | Pan with strong momentum | "Buttery" feel |
    52→| Pinch | Zoom with momentum | Unlike Google Maps |
    53→| Double-tap | Zoom in | Fixed 2x increment |
    54→| Two-finger tap | Zoom out | Fixed 0.5x increment |
    55→| Rotate gesture | Rotate map | Optional feature |
    56→
    57→**Key Differentiator:** iOS implements momentum for BOTH pan AND zoom gestures, creating a more fluid feel than Google Maps.
    58→
    59→### 1.4 Canva
    60→
    61→| Gesture | Action | Notes |
    62→|---------|--------|-------|
    63→| Drag | Pan canvas | Infinite canvas |
    64→| Scroll wheel | Zoom | Centers on cursor |
    65→| Pinch | Zoom | Touch devices |
    66→| Pan gesture | Pan | Two-finger drag |
    67→
    68→**Infinite Canvas:** Canva's whiteboard feature supports unlimited pan in all directions with zoom/pan/scroll navigation.

    72→## 2. Mouse Events Reference
    73→
    74→### 2.1 Event Types
    75→
    76→| Event | Triggers | Properties |
    77→|-------|----------|------------|
    78→| `mousedown` | Button pressed | `button`, `buttons`, `clientX/Y` |
    79→| `mouseup` | Button released | `button`, `buttons`, `clientX/Y` |
    80→| `mousemove` | Cursor moved | `clientX/Y`, `movementX/Y` |
    81→| `click` | Press + release (same element) | Fires after `mouseup` |
    82→| `dblclick` | Two clicks within threshold | ~300ms default |
    83→| `contextmenu` | Right-click | Prevent for custom menu |
    84→| `wheel` | Scroll wheel rotated | `deltaX/Y/Z`, `deltaMode` |
    85→| `mouseenter` | Cursor enters element | Does NOT bubble |
    86→| `mouseleave` | Cursor leaves element | Does NOT bubble |
    87→| `mouseover` | Cursor over element | Bubbles (includes children) |
    88→| `mouseout` | Cursor leaves element | Bubbles (includes children) |
    89→
    90→### 2.2 Button Values
    91→
    92→| `event.button` | Description |
    93→|----------------|-------------|
    94→| 0 | Left button (primary) |
    95→| 1 | Middle button (wheel click) |
    96→| 2 | Right button (secondary) |
    97→| 3 | Back button (browser) |
    98→| 4 | Forward button (browser) |

   100→### 2.3 Drag Implementation Pattern
   101→
   102→```typescript
   103→interface DragState {
   104→  isDragging: boolean;
   105→  startX: number;
   106→  startY: number;
   107→  lastX: number;
   108→  lastY: number;
   109→  velocityX: number;
   110→  velocityY: number;
   111→  lastTime: number;
   112→}
   113→
   114→// Calculate velocity during drag for momentum
   115→function onMouseMove(e: MouseEvent, state: DragState) {
   116→  const now = performance.now();
   117→  const dt = now - state.lastTime;
   118→
   119→  if (dt > 0) {
   120→    state.velocityX = (e.clientX - state.lastX) / dt;
   121→    state.velocityY = (e.clientY - state.lastY) / dt;
   122→  }
   123→
   124→  state.lastX = e.clientX;
   125→  state.lastY = e.clientY;
   126→  state.lastTime = now;
   127→}
   128→```
   129→
   130→### 2.4 Double-Click Detection
   131→
   132→```typescript
   133→const DOUBLE_CLICK_THRESHOLD = 300; // ms
   134→let lastClickTime = 0;
   135→
   136→function onClick(e: MouseEvent) {
   137→  const now = Date.now();
   138→  if (now - lastClickTime < DOUBLE_CLICK_THRESHOLD) {
   139→    handleDoubleClick(e);
   140→  }
   141→  lastClickTime = now;
   142→}
   143→```

   146→
   147→## 3. Touch Events Reference
   148→
   149→### 3.1 Touch Event Types
   150→
   151→| Event | Triggers | Notes |
   152→|-------|----------|-------|
   153→| `touchstart` | Finger touches screen | One or more touches |
   154→| `touchmove` | Finger moves on screen | Fires frequently |
   155→| `touchend` | Finger lifts from screen | |
   156→| `touchcancel` | Touch interrupted | System event, incoming call |
   157→
   158→### 3.2 Pointer Events (Modern, Unified)
   159→
   160→Pointer Events unify mouse, touch, and pen input:
   161→
   162→| Event | Equivalent Touch/Mouse |
   163→|-------|------------------------|
   164→| `pointerdown` | `touchstart` / `mousedown` |
   165→| `pointermove` | `touchmove` / `mousemove` |
   166→| `pointerup` | `touchend` / `mouseup` |
   167→| `pointercancel` | `touchcancel` |
   168→| `pointerenter` | `mouseenter` |
   169→| `pointerleave` | `mouseleave` |
   170→
   171→**Key Properties:**
   172→- `pointerId`: Unique identifier for each contact
   173→- `pointerType`: "mouse", "touch", "pen"
   174→- `isPrimary`: True for first finger/primary button
   175→- `pressure`: 0.0 to 1.0 (pen pressure)
   176→- `width`, `height`: Contact area size
   177→
   178→### 3.3 Pinch-to-Zoom Algorithm
   179→
   180→```typescript
   181→// Cache active pointers
   182→const evCache: PointerEvent[] = [];
   183→let prevDist = -1;
   184→
   185→function onPointerDown(ev: PointerEvent) {
   186→  evCache.push(ev);
   187→}
   188→
   189→function onPointerMove(ev: PointerEvent) {
   190→  // Update cached pointer
   191→  const idx = evCache.findIndex(e => e.pointerId === ev.pointerId);
   192→  if (idx >= 0) evCache[idx] = ev;
   193→
   194→  // Pinch gesture requires exactly 2 pointers
   195→  if (evCache.length === 2) {
   196→    const dx = evCache[0].clientX - evCache[1].clientX;
   197→    const dy = evCache[0].clientY - evCache[1].clientY;
   198→    const curDist = Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);
   199→
   200→    if (prevDist > 0) {
   201→      const scale = curDist / prevDist;
   202→      // scale > 1 = zoom in, scale < 1 = zoom out
   203→      handlePinchZoom(scale, getMidpoint(evCache[0], evCache[1]));
   204→    }
   205→
   206→    prevDist = curDist;
   207→  }
   208→}
   209→
   210→function onPointerUp(ev: PointerEvent) {
   211→  const idx = evCache.findIndex(e => e.pointerId === ev.pointerId);
   212→  if (idx >= 0) evCache.splice(idx, 1);
   213→  if (evCache.length < 2) prevDist = -1;
   214→}
   215→
   216→function getMidpoint(a: PointerEvent, b: PointerEvent) {
   217→  return {
   218→    x: (a.clientX + b.clientX) / 2,
   219→    y: (a.clientY + b.clientY) / 2
   220→  };
   221→}
   222→```
   223→
   224→### 3.4 Touch CSS Requirements
   225→
   226→```css
   227→/* Prevent default browser gestures */
   228→.canvas-container {
   229→  touch-action: none;        /* Full control over touch */
   230→  -webkit-user-select: none; /* Prevent text selection */
   231→  user-select: none;
   232→}
   233→```

   236→
   237→## 4. Wheel Event Normalization
   238→
   239→### 4.1 The Problem
   240→
   241→Different browsers/platforms report wildly different `deltaY` values:
   242→
   243→| Environment | deltaY per "tick" |
   244→|-------------|-------------------|
   245→| Chrome (Windows) | 100-120 |
   246→| Chrome (macOS trackpad) | 1-5 (many events) |
   247→| Firefox (Windows) | 3 (lines mode) |
   248→| Firefox (macOS) | 1-2 |
   249→| Safari | 1-12 |
   250→
   251→### 4.2 deltaMode Values
   252→
   253→```typescript
   254→enum WheelDeltaMode {
   255→  DOM_DELTA_PIXEL = 0,  // deltaY is in pixels
   256→  DOM_DELTA_LINE = 1,   // deltaY is in lines
   257→  DOM_DELTA_PAGE = 2    // deltaY is in pages
   258→}
   259→```
   260→
   261→### 4.3 Facebook's Normalization Algorithm
   262→
   263→```typescript
   264→// Constants for normalization
   265→const PIXEL_STEP = 10;
   266→const LINE_HEIGHT = 40;
   267→const PAGE_HEIGHT = 800;
   268→
   269→interface NormalizedWheel {
   270→  spinX: number;   // Normalized spin (-1 to 1)
   271→  spinY: number;   // Normalized spin (-1 to 1)
   272→  pixelX: number;  // Normalized pixels
   273→  pixelY: number;  // Normalized pixels
   274→}
   275→
   276→function normalizeWheel(event: WheelEvent): NormalizedWheel {
   277→  let sX = 0, sY = 0;   // spin
   278→  let pX = 0, pY = 0;   // pixel
   279→
   280→  // Modern wheel event
   281→  if ('deltaY' in event) {
   282→    pX = event.deltaX;
   283→    pY = event.deltaY;
   284→
   285→    // Convert based on deltaMode
   286→    if (event.deltaMode === 1) {        // LINE mode
   287→      pX *= LINE_HEIGHT;
   288→      pY *= LINE_HEIGHT;
   289→    } else if (event.deltaMode === 2) { // PAGE mode
   290→      pX *= PAGE_HEIGHT;
   291→      pY *= PAGE_HEIGHT;
   292→    }
   293→  }
   294→
   295→  // Legacy fallback
   296→  if ('wheelDelta' in event) {
   297→    const wd = (event as any).wheelDelta;
   298→    if ('wheelDeltaY' in event) {
   299→      sY = -(event as any).wheelDeltaY / 120;
   300→      sX = -(event as any).wheelDeltaX / 120;
   301→    } else {
   302→      sY = -wd / 120;
   303→    }
   304→  }
   305→
   306→  // Calculate spin from pixel if not set
   307→  if (!sX && pX) sX = pX < 0 ? -1 : pX > 0 ? 1 : 0;
   308→  if (!sY && pY) sY = pY < 0 ? -1 : pY > 0 ? 1 : 0;
   309→
   310→  return { spinX: sX, spinY: sY, pixelX: pX, pixelY: pY };
   311→}
   312→```
   313→
   314→### 4.4 Trackpad vs Mouse Detection
   315→
   316→Trackpads produce many small events rapidly; mice produce fewer large events:
   317→
   318→```typescript
   319→let lastWheelTime = 0;
   320→let wheelEventCount = 0;
   321→
   322→function detectInputType(event: WheelEvent): 'trackpad' | 'mouse' {
   323→  const now = performance.now();
   324→  const timeDelta = now - lastWheelTime;
   325→
   326→  if (timeDelta < 50) {
   327→    wheelEventCount++;
   328→  } else {
   329→    wheelEventCount = 1;
   330→  }
   331→
   332→  lastWheelTime = now;
   333→
   334→  // Trackpads fire many events rapidly (<20ms apart)
   335→  // Mice fire discrete events with gaps
   336→  if (wheelEventCount > 3 && timeDelta < 20) {
   337→    return 'trackpad';
   338→  }
   339→
   340→  // Also check delta magnitude
   341→  if (Math.abs(event.deltaY) < 10 && event.deltaMode === 0) {
   342→    return 'trackpad'; // Small pixel deltas = trackpad
   343→  }
   344→
   345→  return 'mouse';
   346→}
   347→```
   348→
   349→---
   350→
   351→## 5. Momentum/Inertia Physics
   352→
   353→### 5.1 Basic Momentum Implementation
   354→
   355→```typescript
   356→interface MomentumState {
   357→  velocityX: number;
   358→  velocityY: number;
   359→  friction: number;
   360→  minVelocity: number;
   361→  animationId: number | null;
   362→}
   363→
   364→const momentum: MomentumState = {
   365→  velocityX: 0,
   366→  velocityY: 0,
   367→  friction: 0.95,      // Per-frame velocity multiplier
   368→  minVelocity: 0.1,    // Stop threshold
   369→  animationId: null
   370→};
   371→
   372→function startMomentum(vx: number, vy: number) {
   373→  momentum.velocityX = vx;
   374→  momentum.velocityY = vy;
   375→
   376→  if (momentum.animationId) {
   377→    cancelAnimationFrame(momentum.animationId);
   378→  }
   379→
   380→  momentum.animationId = requestAnimationFrame(animateMomentum);
   381→}
   382→
   383→function animateMomentum() {
   384→  // Apply velocity to pan position
   385→  panX += momentum.velocityX;
   386→  panY += momentum.velocityY;
   387→
   388→  // Apply friction
   389→  momentum.velocityX *= momentum.friction;
   390→  momentum.velocityY *= momentum.friction;
   391→
   392→  // Stop when velocity is negligible
   393→  const speed = Math.sqrt(
   394→    momentum.velocityX ** 2 + momentum.velocityY ** 2
   395→  );
   396→
   397→  if (speed > momentum.minVelocity) {
   398→    momentum.animationId = requestAnimationFrame(animateMomentum);
   399→  } else {
   400→    momentum.animationId = null;
   401→  }
   402→}
   403→
   404→function stopMomentum() {
   405→  if (momentum.animationId) {
   406→    cancelAnimationFrame(momentum.animationId);
   407→    momentum.animationId = null;
   408→  }
   409→  momentum.velocityX = 0;
   410→  momentum.velocityY = 0;
   411→}
   412→```
   413→
   414→### 5.2 Easing Functions
   415→
   416→```typescript
   417→// Common easing functions for zoom animations
   418→const Easing = {
   419→  // Quadratic ease out (Google Maps style)
   420→  quadOut: (t: number) => t * (2 - t),
   421→
   422→  // Cubic ease out (smoother)
   423→  cubicOut: (t: number) => 1 - Math.pow(1 - t, 3),
   424→
   425→  // Expo ease out (fast start, slow end)
   426→  expoOut: (t: number) => t === 1 ? 1 : 1 - Math.pow(2, -10 * t),
   427→
   428→  // Circ ease out (natural feel)
   429→  circOut: (t: number) => Math.sqrt(1 - Math.pow(t - 1, 2))
   430→};
   431→
   432→function animateZoom(
   433→  fromZoom: number,
   434→  toZoom: number,
   435→  duration: number,
   436→  easing = Easing.quadOut
   437→) {
   438→  const startTime = performance.now();
   439→
   440→  function tick(currentTime: number) {
   441→    const elapsed = currentTime - startTime;
   442→    const progress = Math.min(elapsed / duration, 1);
   443→    const easedProgress = easing(progress);
   444→
   445→    const currentZoom = fromZoom + (toZoom - fromZoom) * easedProgress;
   446→    setZoom(currentZoom);
   447→
   448→    if (progress < 1) {
   449→      requestAnimationFrame(tick);
   450→    }
   451→  }
   452→
   453→  requestAnimationFrame(tick);
   454→}
   455→```
   456→
   457→### 5.3 Recommended Durations
   458→
   459→| Animation | Duration | Easing |
   460→|-----------|----------|--------|
   461→| Zoom step (+/-) | 200-300ms | quadOut |
   462→| Double-click zoom | 300-400ms | cubicOut |
   463→| Reset view | 400-500ms | expoOut |
   464→| Pan deceleration | Physics-based | Friction 0.92-0.95 |
   465→
   466→---
   467→
   468→## 6. Cross-Platform Considerations
   469→
   470→### 6.1 macOS vs Windows Scroll Direction
   471→
   472→| Platform | Default Scroll Direction |
   473→|----------|--------------------------|
   474→| macOS (trackpad) | "Natural" (content follows fingers) |
   475→| macOS (mouse) | Same as trackpad (unless changed) |
   476→| Windows | "Traditional" (opposite of macOS) |
   477→| Linux | Varies by desktop environment |
   478→
   479→**Recommendation:** Use the raw `deltaY` value directly. Browser vendors have already normalized for user preferences. Do NOT attempt to detect or reverse scroll direction.
   480→
   481→### 6.2 Platform-Specific deltaY Magnitude
   482→
   483→| Platform | Typical deltaY (mouse) | Typical deltaY (trackpad) |
   484→|----------|------------------------|---------------------------|
   485→| Windows Chrome | 100 | 1-10 (many events) |
   486→| macOS Chrome | 100 | 0.5-3 (many events) |
   487→| Windows Firefox | 3 (LINE mode) | 1-2 |
   488→| macOS Safari | 12-30 | 0.5-2 |
   489→
   490→### 6.3 Recommended Normalization Strategy
   491→
   492→```typescript
   493→const ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.001;  // Base sensitivity
   494→const MAX_ZOOM_DELTA = 0.5;      // Cap per event
   495→
   496→function calculateZoomDelta(event: WheelEvent): number {
   497→  let delta = event.deltaY;
   498→
   499→  // Normalize LINE mode to pixels
   500→  if (event.deltaMode === 1) {
   501→    delta *= 16; // Approximate line height
   502→  }
   503→
   504→  // Apply sensitivity and clamp
   505→  delta *= ZOOM_SENSITIVITY;
   506→  delta = Math.max(-MAX_ZOOM_DELTA, Math.min(MAX_ZOOM_DELTA, delta));
   507→
   508→  return delta;
   509→}
   510→```
   511→
   512→---
   513→

Visual Mood & Design Philosophy 12→ 13→### 1.1 Design Reference: Linear App 14→ 15→We adopt Linear's visual mood as our design foundation - not its solutions, but its aesthetic sensibility. 16→ 17→ 18→ 19→**What we take from Linear:** 20→- Minimalist, focused interface 21→- Dark mode as default 22→- Generous whitespace 23→- Subtle borders and shadows 24→- Smooth micro-interactions 25→- Typography-first hierarchy 26→ 27→**What we solve differently:** 28→- Railway maintenance planning (not software projects) 29→- Mileage-based scheduling (not sprint-based) 30→- 12-year strategic view (not quarterly roadmaps) 31→- What-if simulation mode (not just viewing) 32→ 33→### 1.2 Moodboard 34→ 35→#### Data Visualization References 36→ 37→ 38→ 39→*Kepler.gl: GPU-accelerated data visualization with deck.gl - our rendering engine* 40→ 41→ 42→ 43→*Flowmap.blue: Clean timeline-based flow visualization* 44→ 45→ 46→ 47→*Microsoft SandDance: Interactive data exploration with smooth transitions* 48→ 49→#### Dashboard & Enterprise UI References 50→ 51→ 52→ 53→*Figma Dark Theme Dashboard: Enterprise-grade dark UI patterns* 54→ 55→ 56→ 57→*Nile Dashboard: Comprehensive dark mode design system* 58→ 59→#### Railway/Maintenance Industry References 60→ 61→ 62→ 63→*Railfleet (Railnova): Competitor reference - fleet planning dashboard with Gantt view* 64→ 65→--- 66→ 67→## 2. Problem → Solution Matrix 68→ 69→Based on RFQ pain points, here's how the demo addresses each: 70→ 71→| # | Client Pain Point (RFQ) | Demo Solution | Screen/Component | 72→|---|------------------------|---------------|------------------| 73→| 1 | No dedicated mileage management tools | Purpose-built mileage dashboard with real-time tracking | Dashboard + KPI Cards | 74→| 2 | IBM Maximo only does asset management | Integrated mileage + maintenance planning | Planning View | 75→| 3 | Manual Excel management | Interactive visual timeline with drag operations | Planning View (Timeline) | 76→| 4 | Folder-driven data storage | Centralized visualization of all fleet data | Sidebar + Data Grid | 77→| 5 | Limited maintenance-operations integration | Single pane of glass for both teams | Unified Planning View | 78→| 6 | Non-compliance risk (underrun/overrun) | Visual alerts + compliance indicators | Alert Banners + Color coding | 79→| 7 | No predictive simulations | **Sandbox Mode** for what-if scenarios | Sandbox Mode (Primary Feature) | 80→| 8 | Insufficient resource visibility | Resource utilization panels | Resource Panel + Heatmap | 81→| 9 | Manual handling delays | Automated scheduling visualization | Timeline + Conflict Detection | 82→ 83→--- 84→ 85→## 3. Design System 86→ 87→### 3.1 Color Palette (Dark Mode) 88→ 89→Inspired by Linear's dark theme with railway-specific semantic colors. 90→ 91→``` 92→BACKGROUND 93→━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 94→bg-primary: #0D0D0D (Pure dark) 95→bg-secondary: #1A1A1A (Elevated surfaces) 96→bg-tertiary: #262626 (Cards, panels) 97→bg-hover: #333333 (Interactive hover) 98→ 99→BORDERS & DIVIDERS 100→━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 101→border-subtle: #2E2E2E (Subtle separation) 102→border-default: #404040 (Default borders) 103→border-strong: #525252 (Emphasized borders) 104→ 105→TEXT 106→━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 107→text-primary: #FFFFFF (Headlines, important) 108→text-secondary: #A3A3A3 (Body text) 109→text-tertiary: #737373 (Labels, captions) 110→text-disabled: #525252 (Disabled states) 111→ 112→SEMANTIC - Visual Mood & Design Philosophy 12→ 13→### 1.1 Design Reference: Linear App 14→ 15→We adopt Linear's visual mood as our design foundation - not its solutions, but its aesthetic sensibility. 16→ 17→ 18→ 19→**What we take from Linear:** 20→- Minimalist, focused interface 21→- Dark mode as default 22→- Generous whitespace 23→- Subtle borders and shadows 24→- Smooth micro-interactions 25→- Typography-first hierarchy 26→ 27→**What we solve differently:** 28→- Railway maintenance planning (not software projects) 29→- Mileage-based scheduling (not sprint-based) 30→- 12-year strategic view (not quarterly roadmaps) 31→- What-if simulation mode (not just viewing) 32→ 33→### 1.2 Moodboard 34→ 35→#### Data Visualization References 36→ 37→ 38→ 39→*Kepler.gl: GPU-accelerated data visualization with deck.gl - our rendering engine* 40→ 41→ 42→ 43→*Flowmap.blue: Clean timeline-based flow visualization* 44→ 45→ 46→ 47→*Microsoft SandDance: Interactive data exploration with smooth transitions* 48→ 49→#### Dashboard & Enterprise UI References 50→ 51→ 52→ 53→*Figma Dark Theme Dashboard: Enterprise-grade dark UI patterns* 54→ 55→ 56→ 57→*Nile Dashboard: Comprehensive dark mode design system* 58→ 59→#### Railway/Maintenance Industry References 60→ 61→ 62→ 63→*Railfleet (Railnova): Competitor reference - fleet planning dashboard with Gantt view* 64→ 65→--- 66→ 67→## 2. Problem → Solution Matrix 68→ 69→Based on RFQ pain points, here's how the demo addresses each: 70→ 71→| # | Client Pain Point (RFQ) | Demo Solution | Screen/Component | 72→|---|------------------------|---------------|------------------| 73→| 1 | No dedicated mileage management tools | Purpose-built mileage dashboard with real-time tracking | Dashboard + KPI Cards | 74→| 2 | IBM Maximo only does asset management | Integrated mileage + maintenance planning | Planning View | 75→| 3 | Manual Excel management | Interactive visual timeline with drag operations | Planning View (Timeline) | 76→| 4 | Folder-driven data storage | Centralized visualization of all fleet data | Sidebar + Data Grid | 77→| 5 | Limited maintenance-operations integration | Single pane of glass for both teams | Unified Planning View | 78→| 6 | Non-compliance risk (underrun/overrun) | Visual alerts + compliance indicators | Alert Banners + Color coding | 79→| 7 | No predictive simulations | **Sandbox Mode** for what-if scenarios | Sandbox Mode (Primary Feature) | 80→| 8 | Insufficient resource visibility | Resource utilization panels | Resource Panel + Heatmap | 81→| 9 | Manual handling delays | Automated scheduling visualization | Timeline + Conflict Detection | 82→ 83→--- 84→ 85→## 3. Verifique o harness e de continuidade, analise os ultimos 2 ultimos commits no git e as ultimas mensagens do claude code salvas (~/.claude/projects/......) para entender o que foi feito corretamente, alucinacões e percas por estouro de contexto. Antes de iniciar tasks intensas, registre essa tarefa no harnes e deixe claro no harness que sempre deve ser salvo os pequenos progressos para evitar que continuemos tendo este tipo de problema por deixar para salvar no haness apenas mudanças grandes, tudo deve ser avaliado se deve ser salvo pensando no que aconteceria se o chat fosse perdido.

    41→
